1	from starlette.middleware.wsgi import (
2	    WSGIMiddleware as WSGIMiddleware,
3	)  # pragma: no cover # noqa
4

1	from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
2	
3	from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send
4	
5	
6	# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed
7	# in their own AsyncExitStack
8	class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
9	    def __init__(
10	        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
11	    ) -> None:
12	        self.app = app
13	        self.context_name = context_name
14	
15	    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
16	        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
17	            scope[self.context_name] = stack
18	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
19

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

11	async def http_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: HTTPException) -> Response:
12	    headers = getattr(exc, "headers", None)
13	    if not is_body_allowed_for_status_code(exc.status_code):
14	        return Response(status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers)
15	    return JSONResponse(
16	        {"detail": exc.detail}, status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers
17	    )
18	
19	
20	async def request_validation_exception_handler(
21	    request: Request, exc: RequestValidationError
22	) -> JSONResponse:
23	    return JSONResponse(
24	        status_code=422,
25	        content={"detail": jsonable_encoder(exc.errors())},
26	    )
27	
28	
29	async def websocket_request_validation_exception_handler(
30	    websocket: WebSocket, exc: WebSocketRequestValidationError
31	) -> None:
32	    await websocket.close(
33	        code=WS_1008_POLICY_VIOLATION, reason=jsonable_encoder(exc.errors())
34	    )

1	from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
2	
3	from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send
4	
5	
6	# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed
7	# in their own AsyncExitStack
8	class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
9	    def __init__(
10	        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
11	    ) -> None:
12	        self.app = app
13	        self.context_name = context_name
14	
15	    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
16	        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
17	            scope[self.context_name] = stack
18	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)

52	
53	                Read more about it in the
54	                [FastAPI docs for Handling Errors](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/#use-httpexception)
55	                """
56	            ),
57	        ],
58	        detail: Annotated[
59	            Any,
60	            Doc(
61	                """
62	                Any data to be sent to the client in the `detail` key of the JSON
63	                response.
64	
65	                Read more about it in the
66	                [FastAPI docs for Handling Errors](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/#use-httpexception)
67	                """
68	            ),
69	        ] = None,
70	        headers: Annotated[
71	            Mapping[str, str] | None,
72	            Doc(
73	                """
74	                Any headers to send to the client in the response.
75	
76	                Read more about it in the
77	                [FastAPI docs for Handling Errors](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/#add-custom-headers)
78	
79	                """
80	            ),
81	        ] = None,
82	    ) -> None:
83	        super().__init__(status_code=status_code, detail=detail, headers=headers)

86	class WebSocketException(StarletteWebSocketException):
87	    """
88	    A WebSocket exception you can raise in your own code to show errors to the client.
89	
90	    This is for client errors, invalid authentication, invalid data, etc. Not for server
91	    errors in your code.
92	
93	    Read more about it in the
94	    [FastAPI docs for WebSockets](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/websockets/).
95	
96	    ## Example
97	
98	    ```python
99	    from typing import Annotated
100	
101	    from fastapi import (
102	        Cookie,
103	        FastAPI,
104	        WebSocket,
105	        WebSocketException,
106	        status,
107	    )
108	
109	    app = FastAPI()
110	
111	    @app.websocket("/items/{item_id}/ws")
112	    async def websocket_endpoint(
113	        *,
114	        websocket: WebSocket,
115	        session: Annotated[str | None, Cookie()] = None,
116	        item_id: str,
117	    ):
118	        if session is None:
119	            raise WebSocketException(code=status.WS_1008_POLICY_VIOLATION)
120	        await websocket.accept()
121	        while True:
122	            data = await websocket.receive_text()
123	            await websocket.send_text(f"Session cookie is: {session}")
124	            await websocket.send_text(f"Message text was: {data}, for item ID: {item_id}")
125	    ```
126	    """
127	
128	    def __init__(
129	        self,
130	        code: Annotated[
131	            int,
132	            Doc(
133	                """
134	                A closing code from the
135	                [valid codes defined in the specification](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6455#section-7.4.1).
136	                """
137	            ),
138	        ],
139	        reason: Annotated[
140	            str | None,
141	            Doc(
142	                """
143	                The reason to close the WebSocket connection.
144	
145	                It is UTF-8-encoded data. The interpretation of the reason is up to the
146	                application, it is not specified by the WebSocket specification.
147	
148	                It could contain text that could be human-readable or interpretable
149	                by the client code, etc.
150	                """
151	            ),
152	        ] = None,
153	    ) -> None:
154	        super().__init__(code=code, reason=reason)
155

174	class ValidationException(Exception):
175	    def __init__(
176	        self,
177	        errors: Sequence[Any],
178	        *,
179	        endpoint_ctx: EndpointContext | None = None,
180	    ) -> None:
181	        self._errors = errors
182	        self.endpoint_ctx = endpoint_ctx
183	
184	        ctx = endpoint_ctx or {}
185	        self.endpoint_function = ctx.get("function")
186	        self.endpoint_path = ctx.get("path")
187	        self.endpoint_file = ctx.get("file")
188	        self.endpoint_line = ctx.get("line")
189	
190	    def errors(self) -> Sequence[Any]:
191	        return self._errors
192	
193	    def _format_endpoint_context(self) -> str:
194	        if not (self.endpoint_file and self.endpoint_line and self.endpoint_function):
195	            if self.endpoint_path:
196	                return f"\n  Endpoint: {self.endpoint_path}"
197	            return ""
198	
199	        context = f'\n  File "{self.endpoint_file}", line {self.endpoint_line}, in {self.endpoint_function}'
200	        if self.endpoint_path:
201	            context += f"\n    {self.endpoint_path}"
202	        return context
203	
204	    def __str__(self) -> str:
205	        message = f"{len(self._errors)} validation error{'s' if len(self._errors) != 1 else ''}:\n"
206	        for err in self._errors:
207	            message += f"  {err}\n"
208	        message += self._format_endpoint_context()
209	        return message.rstrip()
210

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

62	app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)

69	    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16

72	
73	    with client:
74	        with open(path, "rb") as file:
75	            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
76	        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text

7	
8	@pytest.fixture
9	def app():
10	    app = FastAPI()
11	    app.add_middleware(
12	        RateLimitMiddleware,
13	        requests_per_minute=60,
14	        burst_size=5,
15	    )
16	
17	    @app.get("/")
18	    async def root():
19	        return {"message": "hello"}
20	
21	    @app.get("/other")

23	        return {"message": "other"}
24	
25	    return app
26	
27	
28	@pytest.fixture
29	def client(app):
30	    return TestClient(app)
31	
32	
33	class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
34	    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
35	        """Requests under the burst size should succeed."""
36	        for _ in range(5):
37	            response = client.get("/")
38	            assert response.status_code == 200
39	
40	    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
41	        """Requests exceeding burst size should be blocked."""
42	        # Use up the burst
43	        for _ in range(5):
44	            client.get("/")
45	        # Next request should be rate limited
46	        response = client.get("/")
47	        assert response.status_code == 429
48	        body = response.json()
49	        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
50	        assert "retry_after" in body
51	
52	    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
53	        """All responses should include rate limit headers."""
54	        response = client.get("/")
55	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
56	        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
57	        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers
58	
59	    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
60	        """Remaining count should decrease with each request."""
61	        r1 = client.get("/")
62	        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
63	        r2 = client.get("/")
64	        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
65	        assert remaining2 < remaining1
66	
67	    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
68	        """Rate limits should be per-client IP."""
69	        # TestClient always uses the same IP, so this tests the basic flow
70	        response = client.get("/")
71	        assert response.status_code == 200
72	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
73	        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"
74	
75	    def test_different_paths_share_limit(self, client):
76	        """Rate limit applies across all paths for the same client."""
77	        # Use up burst across different endpoints
78	        for _ in range(3):
79	            client.get("/")
80	        for _ in range(2):
81	            client.get("/other")
82	        # Should be rate limited now
83	        response = client.get("/")
84	        assert response.status_code == 429
85	
86	    def test_429_response_format(self, client):
87	        """429 response should have proper JSON format."""
88	        for _ in range(5):
89	            client.get("/")
90	        response = client.get("/")
91	        assert response.status_code == 429
92	        body = response.json()
93	        assert isinstance(body["detail"], str)
94	        assert isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))
95	        assert body["retry_after"] > 0
96	
97	    def test_custom_limits(self):
98	        """Should respect custom rate limit configuration."""
99	        app = FastAPI()
100	        app.add_middleware(
101	            RateLimitMiddleware,
102	            requests_per_minute=120,
103	            burst_size=2,
104	        )
105	
106	        @app.get("/")
107	        async def root():
108	            return {"ok": True}
109	
110	        client = TestClient(app)
111	        # Burst of 2
112	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
113	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
114	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 429

1	# Middleware { #middleware }
2	
3	You can add middleware to **FastAPI** applications.
4	
5	A "middleware" is a function that works with every **request** before it is processed by any specific *path operation*. And also with every **response** before returning it.
6	
7	* It takes each **request** that comes to your application.
8	* It can then do something to that **request** or run any needed code.
9	* Then it passes the **request** to be processed by the rest of the application (by some *path operation*).
10	* It then takes the **response** generated by the application (by some *path operation*).
11	* It can do something to that **response** or run any needed code.
12	* Then it returns the **response**.
13	
14	/// note | Technical Details
15	
16	If you have dependencies with `yield`, the exit code will run *after* the middleware.
17	
18	If there were any background tasks (covered in the [Background Tasks](background-tasks.md) section, you will see it later), they will run *after* all the middleware.
19	
20	///
21	
22	## Create a middleware { #create-a-middleware }
23	
24	To create a middleware you use the decorator `@app.middleware("http")` on top of a function.
25	
26	The middleware function receives:
27	
28	* The `request`.
29	* A function `call_next` that will receive the `request` as a parameter.
30	    * This function will pass the `request` to the corresponding *path operation*.
31	    * Then it returns the `response` generated by the corresponding *path operation*.
32	* You can then further modify the `response` before returning it.
33	
34	{* ../../docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[8:9,11,14] *}
35	
36	/// tip
37	
38	Keep in mind that custom proprietary headers can be added [using the `X-` prefix](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers).
39	
40	But if you have custom headers that you want a client in a browser to be able to see, you need to add them to your CORS configurations ([CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)](cors.md)) using the parameter `expose_headers` documented in [Starlette's CORS docs](https://www.starlette.dev/middleware/#corsmiddleware).
41	
42	///
43	
44	/// note | Technical Details
45	
46	You could also use `from starlette.requests import Request`.
47	
48	**FastAPI** provides it as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
49	
50	///
51	
52	### Before and after the `response` { #before-and-after-the-response }
53	
54	You can add code to be run with the `request`,  before any *path operation* receives it.
55	
56	And also after the `response` is generated, before returning it.
57	
58	For example, you could add a custom header `X-Process-Time` containing the time in seconds that it took to process the request and generate a response:
59	
60	{* ../../docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[10,12:13] *}
61	
62	/// tip
63	
64	Here we use [`time.perf_counter()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.perf_counter) instead of `time.time()` because it can be more precise for these use cases. ðŸ¤“
65	
66	///
67	
68	## Multiple middleware execution order { #multiple-middleware-execution-order }
69	
70	When you add multiple middlewares using either `@app.middleware()` decorator or `app.add_middleware()` method, each new middleware wraps the application, forming a stack. The last middleware added is the *outermost*, and the first is the *innermost*.
71	
72	On the request path, the *outermost* middleware runs first.
73	
74	On the response path, it runs last.
75	
76	For example:
77	
78	```Python
79	app.add_middleware(MiddlewareA)
80	app.add_middleware(MiddlewareB)
81	```
82	
83	This results in the following execution order:
84	
85	* **Request**: MiddlewareB â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ route
86	
87	* **Response**: route â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ MiddlewareB
88	
89	This stacking behavior ensures that middlewares are executed in a predictable and controllable order.
90	
91	## Other middlewares { #other-middlewares }
92	
93	You can later read more about other middlewares in the [Advanced User Guide: Advanced Middleware](../advanced/middleware.md).
94	
95	You will read about how to handle <abbr title="Cross-Origin Resource Sharing">CORS</abbr> with a middleware in the next section.
96

1	# Advanced Middleware { #advanced-middleware }
2	
3	In the main tutorial you read how to add [Custom Middleware](../tutorial/middleware.md) to your application.
4	
5	And then you also read how to handle [CORS with the `CORSMiddleware`](../tutorial/cors.md).
6	
7	In this section we'll see how to use other middlewares.
8	
9	## Adding ASGI middlewares { #adding-asgi-middlewares }
10	
11	As **FastAPI** is based on Starlette and implements the <abbr title="Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface">ASGI</abbr> specification, you can use any ASGI middleware.
12	
13	A middleware doesn't have to be made for FastAPI or Starlette to work, as long as it follows the ASGI spec.
14	
15	In general, ASGI middlewares are classes that expect to receive an ASGI app as the first argument.
16	
17	So, in the documentation for third-party ASGI middlewares they will probably tell you to do something like:
18	
19	```Python
20	from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware
21	
22	app = SomeASGIApp()
23	
24	new_app = UnicornMiddleware(app, some_config="rainbow")
25	```
26	
27	But FastAPI (actually Starlette) provides a simpler way to do it that makes sure that the internal middlewares handle server errors and custom exception handlers work properly.
28	
29	For that, you use `app.add_middleware()` (as in the example for CORS).
30	
31	```Python
32	from fastapi import FastAPI
33	from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware
34	
35	app = FastAPI()
36	
37	app.add_middleware(UnicornMiddleware, some_config="rainbow")
38	```
39	
40	`app.add_middleware()` receives a middleware class as the first argument and any additional arguments to be passed to the middleware.
41	
42	## Integrated middlewares { #integrated-middlewares }
43	
44	**FastAPI** includes several middlewares for common use cases, we'll see next how to use them.
45	
46	/// note | Technical Details
47	
48	For the next examples, you could also use `from starlette.middleware.something import SomethingMiddleware`.
49	
50	**FastAPI** provides several middlewares in `fastapi.middleware` just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available middlewares come directly from Starlette.
51	
52	///
53	
54	## `HTTPSRedirectMiddleware` { #httpsredirectmiddleware }
55	
56	Enforces that all incoming requests must either be `https` or `wss`.
57	
58	Any incoming request to `http` or `ws` will be redirected to the secure scheme instead.
59	
60	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[2,6] *}
61	
62	## `TrustedHostMiddleware` { #trustedhostmiddleware }
63	
64	Enforces that all incoming requests have a correctly set `Host` header, in order to guard against HTTP Host Header attacks.
65	
66	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial002_py310.py hl[2,6:8] *}
67	
68	The following arguments are supported:
69	
70	* `allowed_hosts` - A list of domain names that should be allowed as hostnames. Wildcard domains such as `*.example.com` are supported for matching subdomains. To allow any hostname either use `allowed_hosts=["*"]` or omit the middleware.
71	* `www_redirect` - If set to True, requests to non-www versions of the allowed hosts will be redirected to their www counterparts. Defaults to `True`.
72	
73	If an incoming request does not validate correctly then a `400` response will be sent.
74	
75	## `GZipMiddleware` { #gzipmiddleware }
76	
77	Handles GZip responses for any request that includes `"gzip"` in the `Accept-Encoding` header.
78	
79	The middleware will handle both standard and streaming responses.
80	
81	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial003_py310.py hl[2,6] *}
82	
83	The following arguments are supported:
84	
85	* `minimum_size` - Do not GZip responses that are smaller than this minimum size in bytes. Defaults to `500`.
86	* `compresslevel` - Used during GZip compression. It is an integer ranging from 1 to 9. Defaults to `9`. Lower value results in faster compression but larger file sizes, while higher value results in slower compression but smaller file sizes.
87	
88	## Other middlewares { #other-middlewares }
89	
90	There are many other ASGI middlewares.
91	
92	For example:
93	
94	* [Uvicorn's `ProxyHeadersMiddleware`](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/master/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py)
95	* [MessagePack](https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi)
96	
97	To see other available middlewares check [Starlette's Middleware docs](https://www.starlette.dev/middleware/) and the [ASGI Awesome List](https://github.com/florimondmanca/awesome-asgi).
98

5	app = FastAPI()

8	@app.middleware("http")
9	async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
10	    start_time = time.perf_counter()
11	    response = await call_next(request)
12	    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
13	    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)

Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the FastAPI repository. Let me compile the report:

## FastAPI Repository Structure & Conventions Report

### 1. **Project Structure Overview**

The FastAPI repository is organized as follows:

```
fastapi/
â”œâ”€â”€ __init__.py                    # Main exports
â”œâ”€â”€ applications.py                # FastAPI main class (inherits from Starlette)
â”œâ”€â”€ routing.py                     # Route handling and decorators
â”œâ”€â”€ requests.py                    # Request wrappers
â”œâ”€â”€ responses.py                   # Response classes
â”œâ”€â”€ exceptions.py                  # Custom exception classes
â”œâ”€â”€ exception_handlers.py           # Exception handler implementations
â”œâ”€â”€ middleware/                    # Middleware components
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ asyncexitstack.py         # AsyncExitStack middleware
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ cors.py                   # CORS (re-exported from Starlette)
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ gzip.py                   # GZip middleware
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ httpsredirect.py          # HTTPS redirect
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ trustedhost.py            # Trusted host
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ wsgi.py                   # WSGI middleware
â”œâ”€â”€ security/                      # Security features
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ api_key.py
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ http.py
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ oauth2.py
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ base.py
â”œâ”€â”€ dependencies/                  # Dependency injection system
â”œâ”€â”€ openapi/                       # OpenAPI/Swagger documentation
â”œâ”€â”€ encoders.py                    # Custom encoders
â”œâ”€â”€ types.py                       # Type definitions
â””â”€â”€ datastructures.py              # Data structures (re-exports from Starlette)
```

**Key configuration** (pyproject.toml):
- Python >= 3.10
- Core dependencies: Starlette >= 0.46.0, Pydantic >= 2.9.0, typing-extensions >= 4.8.0
- Uses Pydantic v2 (not v1)
- Strict type checking enabled in mypy

### 2. **Top-Level Exports** (`fastapi/__init__.py`)

FastAPI follows an **explicit export pattern** using `as` imports:

```python
from starlette import status as status
from .applications import FastAPI as FastAPI
from .responses import Response as Response
from .responses import JSONResponse as JSONResponse
from .param_functions import Body, Cookie, Depends, File, Form, Header, Path, Query, Security
from .requests import Request as Request
from .websockets import WebSocket as WebSocket
from .exceptions import HTTPException as HTTPException
# ... etc
```

**Convention**: Only public APIs are explicitly exported. Unused imports in `__init__.py` files are ignored via ruff config: `"__init__.py" = ["F401"]`

### 3. **Key Starlette Imports & Patterns**

FastAPI heavily wraps/re-exports Starlette types:

**Core Starlette Types Used:**
- `from starlette.requests import Request, HTTPConnection`
- `from starlette.responses import Response, JSONResponse, StreamingResponse, HTMLResponse, FileResponse`
- `from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send, AppType, Lifespan, ExceptionHandler`
- `from starlette.middleware import Middleware`
- `from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware`
- `from starlette.exceptions import HTTPException, WebSocketException`
- `from starlette.datastructures import State, Headers, QueryParams, FormData, URL, UploadFile`
- `from starlette.websockets import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect`
- `from starlette.concurrency import run_in_threadpool, iterate_in_threadpool`

**Key Files with Starlette Imports:**
- `/fastapi/routing.py` - Most comprehensive Starlette usage (lines 75-91)
- `/fastapi/applications.py` - Application setup with Starlette base
- `/fastapi/exception_handlers.py` - Error handling with HTTPException and JSONResponse
- `/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py` - Dependency resolution

### 4. **JSONResponse Usage Pattern**

JSONResponse is used with this pattern:

```python
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

# Basic usage with status code and content
return JSONResponse(
    {"detail": exc.detail}, 
    status_code=exc.status_code, 
    headers=headers
)

# With jsonable_encoder for complex types
return JSONResponse(
    status_code=422,
    content={"detail": jsonable_encoder(exc.errors())}
)
```

**Default Response Class**: JSONResponse is the default response_class for all route decorators (see routing.py line 355+, applications.py line 375)

**Response Classes Available** (from `fastapi/responses.py`):
- JSONResponse (from Starlette)
- HTMLResponse, PlainTextResponse, RedirectResponse, FileResponse, StreamingResponse
- EventSourceResponse (custom FastAPI implementation)
- ORJSONResponse, UJSONResponse (deprecated)

### 5. **Existing Rate Limiting/Throttling**

**Finding**: No existing rate limiting or throttling implementations found in the codebase.

Searched files for "rate", "limit", "throttle" patterns - results show only mentions in:
- Documentation examples
- Type hints and security-related parameters
- No middleware or decorator implementing rate limiting

### 6. **Exception Handling Patterns**

**Custom Exception Structure** (`exception_handlers.py`):
```python
async def http_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: HTTPException) -> Response:
    headers = getattr(exc, "headers", None)
    if not is_body_allowed_for_status_code(exc.status_code):
        return Response(status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers)
    return JSONResponse(
        {"detail": exc.detail}, status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers
    )
```

**Exception Types**:
- `HTTPException` (extends Starlette's HTTPException)
- `RequestValidationError` (for validation errors)
- `WebSocketRequestValidationError`
- `FastAPIError` (base for FastAPI-specific errors)

### 7. **Middleware Pattern**

**Middleware Implementation Pattern** (AsyncExitStackMiddleware example):
```python
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Middleware Registration**: Added to FastAPI app via:
- `app.add_middleware(MiddlewareClass, **kwargs)`
- Or passed in routes list during initialization

### 8. **Type Annotation Conventions**

FastAPI uses strict typing throughout:

**Type Patterns**:
```python
# Union types using | (Python 3.10+)
response_class: type[Response] | DefaultPlaceholder = Default(JSONResponse)

# Annotated for documentation
path_param: Annotated[str, Path(..., description="...")]

# Type variables for generics
AppType = TypeVar("AppType", bound="FastAPI")

# Callable types
DecoratedCallable = TypeVar("DecoratedCallable", bound=Callable[..., Any])
```

### 9. **Routing & Request/Response Flow**

**Key Pattern** (from routing.py):
- Routes are async-first with `is_async_callable()` checks
- Sync functions are wrapped with `run_in_threadpool()`
- Dependencies are resolved via `solve_dependencies()` 
- AsyncExitStack context available in scope
- Responses can be:
  - Awaitable[Response] 
  - Response object
  - JSON-serializable Python objects (Pydantic models, dicts)

### 10. **Import Organization Convention**

Standard order observed across all files:
1. Standard library imports
2. Third-party imports (anyio, pydantic, starlette)
3. Local imports (from fastapi.*)
4. Type hints and annotations last

**Important**: Files use `# noqa: F401` comments for intentional re-exports in `__init__.py` files.

## Summary for Rate Limiting Implementation

Based on this analysis, for implementing a rate limiter you should:

1. **Create a custom middleware class** similar to `AsyncExitStackMiddleware` implementing `ASGIApp` protocol
2. **Inherit exception patterns** - Return `JSONResponse` with proper status codes (429 for rate limit exceeded)
3. **Use Starlette types**: `Scope`, `Receive`, `Send`, `ASGIApp`
4. **Follow typing conventions**: Use Annotated for documentation, | for unions, strict type hints
5. **Integration points**: Middleware can be added via `app.add_middleware()` or FastAPI's middleware parameter
6. **Status codes**: Use `starlette.status` module for standard codes (429 for rate limit)

### 2. **Top-Level Exports** (`fastapi/__init__.py`)

FastAPI follows an **explicit export pattern** using `as` imports:

```python

```

**Convention**: Only public APIs are explicitly exported. Unused imports in `__init__.py` files are ignored via ruff config: `"__init__.py" = ["F401"]`

### 3. **Key Starlette Imports & Patterns**

FastAPI heavily wraps/re-exports Starlette types:

**Core Starlette Types Used:**

- `from starlette.websockets import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect`
- `from starlette.concurrency import run_in_threadpool, iterate_in_threadpool`

**Key Files with Starlette Imports:**
- `/fastapi/routing.py` - Most comprehensive Starlette usage (lines 75-91)
- `/fastapi/applications.py` - Application setup with Starlette base
- `/fastapi/exception_handlers.py` - Error handling with HTTPException and JSONResponse
- `/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py` - Dependency resolution

### 4. **JSONResponse Usage Pattern**

JSONResponse is used with this pattern:

```python
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

# Basic usage with status code and content
return JSONResponse(
    {"detail": exc.detail}, 
    status_code=exc.status_code, 
    headers=headers
)

# With jsonable_encoder for complex types
return JSONResponse(
    status_code=422,
    content={"detail": jsonable_encoder(exc.errors())}
)
```

**Default Response Class**: JSONResponse is the default response_class for all route decorators (see routing.py line 355+, applications.py line 375)

**Response Classes Available** (from `fastapi/responses.py`):

### 5. **Existing Rate Limiting/Throttling**

**Finding**: No existing rate limiting or throttling implementations found in the codebase.

### 6. **Exception Handling Patterns**

**Custom Exception Structure** (`exception_handlers.py`):
```python
async def http_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: HTTPException) -> Response:
    headers = getattr(exc, "headers", None)
    if not is_body_allowed_for_status_code(exc.status_code):
        return Response(status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers)
    return JSONResponse(
        {"detail": exc.detail}, status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers
    )
```

**Exception Types**:

- `RequestValidationError` (for validation errors)
- `WebSocketRequestValidationError`
- `FastAPIError` (base for FastAPI-specific errors)

### 7. **Middleware Pattern**

**Middleware Implementation Pattern** (AsyncExitStackMiddleware example):
```python
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Middleware Registration**: Added to FastAPI app via:
- `app.add_middleware(MiddlewareClass, **kwargs)`
- Or passed in routes list during initialization

### 8. **Type Annotation Conventions**

FastAPI uses strict typing throughout:

**Type Patterns**:
```python
# Union types using | (Python 3.10+)
response_class: type[Response] | DefaultPlaceholder = Default(JSONResponse)

# Annotated for documentation
path_param: Annotated[str, Path(..., description="...")]

# Type variables for generics
AppType = TypeVar("AppType", bound="FastAPI")

# Callable types
DecoratedCallable = TypeVar("DecoratedCallable", bound=Callable[..., Any])
```

### 9. **Routing & Request/Response Flow**

**Key Pattern** (from routing.py):
- Routes are async-first with `is_async_callable()` checks
- Sync functions are wrapped with `run_in_threadpool()`
- Dependencies are resolved via `solve_dependencies()` 
- AsyncExitStack context available in scope
- Responses can be:
  - Awaitable[Response] 
  - Response object
  - JSON-serializable Python objects (Pydantic models, dicts)

### 10. **Import Organization Convention**

Standard order observed across all files:
1. Standard library imports
2. Third-party imports (anyio, pydantic, starlette)
3. Local imports (from fastapi.*)
4. Type hints and annotations last

**Important**: Files use `# noqa: F401` comments for intentional re-exports in `__init__.py` files.

## Summary for Rate Limiting Implementation

Based on this analysis, for implementing a rate limiter you should:

1. **Create a custom middleware class** similar to `AsyncExitStackMiddleware` implementing `ASGIApp` protocol
2. **Inherit exception patterns** - Return `JSONResponse` with proper status codes (429 for rate limit exceeded)
3. **Use Starlette types**: `Scope`, `Receive`, `Send`, `ASGIApp`
4. **Follow typing conventions**: Use Annotated for documentation, | for unions, strict type hints
5. **Integration points**: Middleware can be added via `app.add_middleware()` or FastAPI's middleware parameter
6. **Status codes**: Use `starlette.status` module for standard codes (429 for rate limit)

### 4. **Testing Framework & TestClient Usage**

```

### 6. **Project Test Configuration**

[tool.pytest]

]

]

**No dedicated conftest.py at root level** - Tests are structured independently with inline test setup.

**Test Dependencies** (from `pyproject.toml`):
- pytest >=9.0.0

- inline-snapshot >=0.21.1 (for snapshot testing)
- pytest-xdist (for distributed testing)
- pytest-cov (for coverage)
- pytest-timeout (enforces 20s timeout)

### 7. **Key Test Patterns Observed**

1. **Inline app definitions**: Tests define FastAPI apps directly or import from `docs_src/`
2. **Module-level TestClient**: `client = TestClient(app)` defined at module level, reused across tests
3. **Parametrized tests**: Using `@pytest.mark.parametrize` for multiple scenarios

### 8. **Relevant File Paths**

Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my findings into a detailed report.

## Comprehensive FastAPI Middleware Implementation Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the FastAPI codebase, here's the complete picture of how middleware is implemented:

### Directory Structure of `/fastapi/middleware/`

The middleware directory contains the following files:
```
/fastapi/middleware/
â”œâ”€â”€ __init__.py                 (58 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ asyncexitstack.py          (637 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ cors.py                    (79 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ gzip.py                    (79 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ httpsredirect.py           (115 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ trustedhost.py             (109 bytes)
â””â”€â”€ wsgi.py                    (107 bytes)
```

### Contents of `__init__.py`

The file exports a re-export from Starlette:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

### All Middleware Files and Their Purpose

1. **cors.py** - Re-exports Starlette's CORSMiddleware
2. **gzip.py** - Re-exports Starlette's GZipMiddleware
3. **httpsredirect.py** - Re-exports Starlette's HTTPSRedirectMiddleware
4. **trustedhost.py** - Re-exports Starlette's TrustedHostMiddleware
5. **wsgi.py** - Re-exports Starlette's WSGIMiddleware
6. **asyncexitstack.py** - FastAPI's custom middleware implementation

### FastAPI-Specific Middleware Implementation Pattern

**The only custom middleware in FastAPI is `AsyncExitStackMiddleware`**, which demonstrates the standard ASGI middleware pattern:

```python
class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Key Implementation Details:**
- **Base class pattern**: Middleware classes don't inherit from any base class - they follow the ASGI callable protocol
- **`__init__` signature**: Takes the ASGI app as the first argument, followed by optional configuration parameters
- **`__call__` method**: Signature is `async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None`
- **Request/Response flow**: The middleware receives ASGI scope, receive, and send objects and must await `self.app(scope, receive, send)` to pass control to the next middleware/handler

### How Middleware is Registered with FastAPI

There are **two primary methods** to add middleware to a FastAPI app:

**Method 1: Using the `@app.middleware("http")` decorator** (for simple middleware)
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

This decorator internally uses `app.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)`.

**Method 2: Using `app.add_middleware()` method** (for ASGI middleware classes)
```python
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=["*"])
app.add_middleware(GZipMiddleware, minimum_size=1000)
```

### Middleware Registration Architecture

The FastAPI class (which extends Starlette) implements the following:

1. **Storage**: Middleware is stored in `self.user_middleware: list[Middleware]` - a list of `Middleware` objects (namedtuples from Starlette)
2. **Middleware object structure**: Each middleware is stored as `Middleware(MiddlewareClass, *args, **kwargs)`
3. **Building the stack**: The `build_middleware_stack()` method constructs the actual middleware chain in this order (from outer to inner):
   - ServerErrorMiddleware (FastAPI adds this first)
   - User middlewares (added via `add_middleware()`)
   - ExceptionMiddleware
   - AsyncExitStackMiddleware (FastAPI-specific)
   
4. **Stack construction**: Middlewares are instantiated in reverse order in `build_middleware_stack()`:
   ```python
   app = self.router
   for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
       app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)  # Each middleware wraps the previous layer
   ```

### Execution Order

When multiple middlewares are added:
```python
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareA)
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareB)
```

**Request path**: MiddlewareB â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ route  
**Response path**: route â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ MiddlewareB

(Last added middleware is the outermost layer, first added is innermost)

### Custom Middleware Example from Tests

The codebase includes a custom middleware example that demonstrates the full ASGI pattern:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
```

### Key Design Characteristics

1. **ASGI-compliant**: All middleware follows the ASGI specification and doesn't require inheritance
2. **Composition-based**: Middleware works by wrapping the ASGI app (not inheritance)
3. **Request/Response filtering**: Middleware can intercept and modify `scope`, `receive`, and `send` objects
4. **BaseHTTPMiddleware bridge**: FastAPI provides `BaseHTTPMiddleware` from Starlette for simpler function-based middleware using the `@app.middleware()` decorator
5. **Backward compatibility**: Most built-in middleware is re-exported from Starlette for convenience

### File Paths

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my findings into a detailed report.

## Comprehensive FastAPI Middleware Implementation Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the FastAPI codebase, here's the complete picture of how middleware is implemented:

### Directory Structure of `/fastapi/middleware/`

The middleware directory contains the following files:
```
/fastapi/middleware/
â”œâ”€â”€ __init__.py                 (58 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ asyncexitstack.py          (637 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ cors.py                    (79 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ gzip.py                    (79 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ httpsredirect.py           (115 bytes)
â”œâ”€â”€ trustedhost.py             (109 bytes)
â””â”€â”€ wsgi.py                    (107 bytes)
```

### Contents of `__init__.py`

The file exports a re-export from Starlette:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

### All Middleware Files and Their Purpose

1. **cors.py** - Re-exports Starlette's CORSMiddleware
2. **gzip.py** - Re-exports Starlette's GZipMiddleware
3. **httpsredirect.py** - Re-exports Starlette's HTTPSRedirectMiddleware
4. **trustedhost.py** - Re-exports Starlette's TrustedHostMiddleware
5. **wsgi.py** - Re-exports Starlette's WSGIMiddleware
6. **asyncexitstack.py** - FastAPI's custom middleware implementation

### FastAPI-Specific Middleware Implementation Pattern

**The only custom middleware in FastAPI is `AsyncExitStackMiddleware`**, which demonstrates the standard ASGI middleware pattern:

```python
class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Key Implementation Details:**
- **Base class pattern**: Middleware classes don't inherit from any base class - they follow the ASGI callable protocol
- **`__init__` signature**: Takes the ASGI app as the first argument, followed by optional configuration parameters
- **`__call__` method**: Signature is `async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None`
- **Request/Response flow**: The middleware receives ASGI scope, receive, and send objects and must await `self.app(scope, receive, send)` to pass control to the next middleware/handler

### How Middleware is Registered with FastAPI

There are **two primary methods** to add middleware to a FastAPI app:

**Method 1: Using the `@app.middleware("http")` decorator** (for simple middleware)
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

This decorator internally uses `app.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)`.

**Method 2: Using `app.add_middleware()` method** (for ASGI middleware classes)
```python
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=["*"])
app.add_middleware(GZipMiddleware, minimum_size=1000)
```

### Middleware Registration Architecture

The FastAPI class (which extends Starlette) implements the following:

1. **Storage**: Middleware is stored in `self.user_middleware: list[Middleware]` - a list of `Middleware` objects (namedtuples from Starlette)
2. **Middleware object structure**: Each middleware is stored as `Middleware(MiddlewareClass, *args, **kwargs)`
3. **Building the stack**: The `build_middleware_stack()` method constructs the actual middleware chain in this order (from outer to inner):
   - ServerErrorMiddleware (FastAPI adds this first)
   - User middlewares (added via `add_middleware()`)
   - ExceptionMiddleware
   - AsyncExitStackMiddleware (FastAPI-specific)
   
4. **Stack construction**: Middlewares are instantiated in reverse order in `build_middleware_stack()`:
   ```python
   app = self.router
   for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
       app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)  # Each middleware wraps the previous layer
   ```

### Execution Order

When multiple middlewares are added:
```python
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareA)
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareB)
```

**Request path**: MiddlewareB â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ route  
**Response path**: route â†’ MiddlewareA â†’ MiddlewareB

(Last added middleware is the outermost layer, first added is innermost)

### Custom Middleware Example from Tests

The codebase includes a custom middleware example that demonstrates the full ASGI pattern:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
```

### Key Design Characteristics

1. **ASGI-compliant**: All middleware follows the ASGI specification and doesn't require inheritance
2. **Composition-based**: Middleware works by wrapping the ASGI app (not inheritance)
3. **Request/Response filtering**: Middleware can intercept and modify `scope`, `receive`, and `send` objects
4. **BaseHTTPMiddleware bridge**: FastAPI provides `BaseHTTPMiddleware` from Starlette for simpler function-based middleware using the `@app.middleware()` decorator
5. **Backward compatibility**: Most built-in middleware is re-exported from Starlette for convenience

### File Paths

- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/fastapi/applications.py` (contains `build_middleware_stack()` and middleware decorator)
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py` (decorator example)
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py` (custom ASGI middleware example)

file.content:
from pathlib import Path

from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()

router = APIRouter()


class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
    Args:
      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
    """

    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0

        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message  # pragma: no cover

            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(
                    422,
                    detail={
                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                        "code": 999,
                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
                    },
                )
            return message

        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return

        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)


@router.post("/middleware")
def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
    return {"message": "OK"}


app.include_router(router)
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)


client = TestClient(app)


def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }


def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}

Design a detailed implementation plan for adding a RateLimitMiddleware to the FastAPI codebase.

## Context

The FastAPI repo is at: /private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390830400-0jehdv/

### Existing Middleware Pattern
All custom middleware in FastAPI follows the raw ASGI protocol (no base class inheritance):

```python
# From fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py - the ONLY custom middleware in the repo
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

Other middleware files (cors.py, gzip.py, etc.) are simple re-exports from Starlette:
```python
# cors.py
from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware as CORSMiddleware  # noqa
```

### `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py` currently only contains:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

### Testing Pattern
- Tests use `from fastapi.testclient import TestClient`
- Test apps are defined inline in test files or imported from docs_src/
- No `tests/test_apps/` directory exists currently
- pytest with 20s timeout, warnings-as-errors
- Module-level TestClient: `client = TestClient(app)`
- Example test (from tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py):
  - App and middleware defined inline
  - `app.add_middleware(MiddlewareClass, **kwargs)` to register
  - `client = TestClient(app)` at module level
  - Tests assert response.status_code, response.json(), response.headers

### JSONResponse Pattern
```python
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
return JSONResponse({"detail": exc.detail}, status_code=exc.status_code, headers=headers)
```

## Requirements

1. **New file `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`** implementing `RateLimitMiddleware`:
   - Follow the ASGI middleware pattern (no base class, takes `app: ASGIApp` as first __init__ param)
   - Config: `requests_per_minute` (int, default 60), `burst_size` (int, default 10)
   - Token bucket algorithm per-client IP
   - Return HTTP 429 with JSON body `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded", "retry_after": <seconds>}` when limit exceeded
   - Add response headers to ALL responses: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
   - Only process `scope["type"] == "http"` requests (pass through websockets/lifespan)

2. **Export from `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`**: Add the RateLimitMiddleware export

3. **Test app at `tests/test_apps/test_rate_limit_app.py`**: Demonstrates middleware usage

4. **All existing tests must still pass**

## Design Questions to Address

1. **Token Bucket Algorithm**: Design the per-IP token bucket. How to store state (dict of IP -> bucket), how tokens refill over time, how burst_size relates to requests_per_minute.
2. **ASGI-level response header injection**: Since this is raw ASGI (not BaseHTTPMiddleware), we need to intercept the `send` callable to inject headers into the HTTP response start message. Design this carefully.
3. **Client IP extraction**: How to get client IP from ASGI scope (scope["client"][0] if scope.get("client")).
4. **Thread safety**: The middleware runs in async context. Consider if we need locks for the token bucket state.
5. **X-RateLimit-Reset calculation**: How many seconds until the bucket would refill to full.
6. **Memory management**: Consider cleanup of stale IP entries.
7. **Test structure**: Design tests that verify rate limiting works, headers are present, 429 is returned correctly.

Please produce a detailed implementation plan with:
- Exact file paths and what goes in each
- Token bucket algorithm pseudocode
- ASGI send wrapper design for header injection
- Test scenarios

3. **Client IP extraction**: How to get client IP from ASGI scope (scope["client"][0] if scope.get("client")).
4. **Thread safety**: The middleware runs in async context. Consider if we need locks for the token bucket state.
5. **X-RateLimit-Reset calculation**: How many seconds until the bucket would refill to full.
6. **Memory management**: Consider cleanup of stale IP entries.
7. **Test structure**: Design tests that verify rate limiting works, headers are present, 429 is returned correctly.

Please produce a detailed implementation plan with:
- Exact file paths and what goes in each
- Token bucket algorithm pseudocode
- ASGI send wrapper design for header injection
- Test scenarios

6	# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed

8	class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
9	    def __init__(
10	        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
11	    ) -> None:
12	        self.app = app
13	        self.context_name = context_name

18	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

33	class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
34	    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
35	        """Requests under the burst size should succeed."""
36	        for _ in range(5):
37	            response = client.get("/")
38	            assert response.status_code == 200
39	
40	    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
41	        """Requests exceeding burst size should be blocked."""
42	        # Use up the burst
43	        for _ in range(5):
44	            client.get("/")
45	        # Next request should be rate limited
46	        response = client.get("/")
47	        assert response.status_code == 429
48	        body = response.json()
49	        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
50	        assert "retry_after" in body
51	
52	    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
53	        """All responses should include rate limit headers."""
54	        response = client.get("/")
55	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
56	        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
57	        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers
58	
59	    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
60	        """Remaining count should decrease with each request."""
61	        r1 = client.get("/")
62	        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
63	        r2 = client.get("/")
64	        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
65	        assert remaining2 < remaining1
66	
67	    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
68	        """Rate limits should be per-client IP."""
69	        # TestClient always uses the same IP, so this tests the basic flow
70	        response = client.get("/")
71	        assert response.status_code == 200
72	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
73	        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"
74	
75	    def test_different_paths_share_limit(self, client):
76	        """Rate limit applies across all paths for the same client."""
77	        # Use up burst across different endpoints
78	        for _ in range(3):
79	            client.get("/")
80	        for _ in range(2):
81	            client.get("/other")
82	        # Should be rate limited now
83	        response = client.get("/")
84	        assert response.status_code == 429
85	
86	    def test_429_response_format(self, client):
87	        """429 response should have proper JSON format."""
88	        for _ in range(5):
89	            client.get("/")
90	        response = client.get("/")
91	        assert response.status_code == 429
92	        body = response.json()
93	        assert isinstance(body["detail"], str)
94	        assert isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))
95	        assert body["retry_after"] > 0
96	
97	    def test_custom_limits(self):
98	        """Should respect custom rate limit configuration."""
99	        app = FastAPI()
100	        app.add_middleware(
101	            RateLimitMiddleware,
102	            requests_per_minute=120,
103	            burst_size=2,
104	        )
105	
106	        @app.get("/")
107	        async def root():
108	            return {"ok": True}
109	
110	        client = TestClient(app)
111	        # Burst of 2
112	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
113	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
114	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 429

2. **`test_blocks_requests_over_burst`**: After 5 requests, the 6th is blocked (429). Body must have `"detail"` containing `"Rate limit exceeded"` and a `"retry_after"` key.

8. **`test_custom_limits`**: `burst_size=2`, so 2 requests succeed, 3rd is 429. `X-RateLimit-Limit` will be `"120"`.

And from test line 93-94: `body["detail"]` is `isinstance(..., str)` and `body["retry_after"]` is a number > 0.
